front: Painting: couples preparing to depart an island, surrounded by swarms of putti near a golden gondola
Painting: two versions exist; the Charlottenburg Palace version has a mast in the background unlike the Louvre original
Painting: debate over whether figures are leaving or arriving at the birthplace of Venus
Painting: reception piece that ended the Poussiniste-Rubeniste debate at the Academy in 1717
Painting: inspired by Dancourt's play The Three Cousins; students threw breadcrumbs at it during the French Revolution
Painting: inspired Baudelaire's poem 'A Voyage to Cythera' and William Kentridge's 1980s charcoal series
Painting: workers packing a portrait of Louis XIV into a wooden box in an art dealer's shop
Painting: intended as an advertisement for the art dealer Edme Gersaint; last major work before death from tuberculosis
Painting: features contrast between older narrow-waist gowns and fashionable sack-back gowns; Pierre Mignard portrait of Louis XIV being removed
Painting: forlorn figure in white commedia dell'arte costume with pink/red bows on shoes
Painting: Pantalon rides a donkey in background; Leandro and Isabella also look on
Painting: Dora Panofsky argued this painting's pose derives from Christ in Rembrandt's 1665 Ecce Homo
Painting: allegedly sold by junk dealer with label 'Buy me, I'm so lonely!'; may have been sign for cafe of actor Belloni
Painting: commedia dell'arte character plays guitar; backwards-facing female statue behind him
Painting: man reads sheet music from a woman's lap while playing guitar; bust possibly of Pythagoras in background
back: The Scale of Love / The Love Song (Antoine Watteau)
work: The Love Lesson / The Scale of Love / The Love Song

front: Artist: French Academy created the term 'fete galante' in 1717 specifically for this artist's paintings of dressed-up nobility in imaginary parks
Artist: used the trois-crayons (three chalks) technique for drawings
Artist: studied under Claude Audran at the Luxembourg Palace, copying scenes from Rubens's Marie de' Medici cycle
Artist: beaten by Antoine Grison in 1709 attempt to win the Prix de Rome
Artist: a type of pleat named after this artist due to frequent appearance in his paintings
Artist: leading artist of singerie (satirical genre with monkeys performing human activities)
Painting: depicts a sickle-wielding Ceres with a crayfish and lion; only surviving work from a four seasons series
Painting: blue-and-white-striped Tuscan columns support a portico; crowd watches a dancing couple
back: The Pleasures of the Dance (Antoine Watteau)
